aliases: Pasta in Tomato Sauce with Meatballs

english: Pasta in Tomato Sauce with Meatballs

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for meatball pasta; 'pasta dish' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest defensible broader class. A more specific term for pasta with meat sauce or meatballs is not available in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers pasta dishes broadly; no specific code for meatball pasta exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for meatball pasta as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of meatball pasta is approximately 300 g (one plate), comprising pasta, 3–4 meatballs, and tomato-based sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Meatball Pasta — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Meatball Pasta (cr_id F05060078), confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta and pasta-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 49 (low) is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for white pasta with tomato-based meat sauce, which is the closest analogous food to meatball pasta. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (49 × 33.87) / 100 ≈ 16.6, rounded to 16.5 for a 300 g serving (11.29 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3 = 33.87 g). Input record confidence for GI was 0.536, reflecting analogous estimation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 272 mg per 100 g; estimated 816 mg per 300 g serving, approaching 35% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, targeting sodium below 200 mg per 100 g, and pair the dish with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 11.29 g per 100 g with only 1.2 g dietary fibre, yielding a low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio of 0.11 that limits attenuation of postprandial glucose rise; GI 49 (low) partially mitigates risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute refined pasta with wholemeal or legume-based pasta to raise dietary fibre above 3 g per 100 g, reduce portion size to 150–200 g, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to lower the overall glycaemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef or pork meatballs (estimated 1.5 g per 100 g) and sodium at 272 mg per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; total fat 4.29 g per 100 g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200 g, choose lean mince (less than 10% fat) for meatballs, request low-sodium tomato sauce, and increase vegetable content to improve the overall dietary fibre and micronutrient profile of the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef and pork meatballs are rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); refined pasta contributes to glycaemic load, further driving systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef or pork meatballs with turkey, chicken, or plant-based alternatives to reduce arachidonic acid intake, use wholemeal pasta, and monitor individual tolerance to nightshade tomato sauce for joint symptom flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Meatball Pasta is a Western-style prepared dish consisting of boiled pasta (typically spaghetti or penne) served with beef or pork meatballs in a tomato-based sauce seasoned with onion, garlic, herbs, and salt. The dish is widely consumed in Singapore as a working-lunch option and is classified under grains and staples, pasta-based dishes. Per 100 g, it provides 109 kcal, 6.29 g protein, 4.29 g fat, and 11.29 g carbohydrate, with 1.2 g dietary fibre and 272 mg sodium. The texture is soft and hearty, with a savoury, mildly acidic tomato flavour balanced by the richness of the meat. Clinically, the dish carries moderate concern for hypertension due to its borderline sodium content, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio despite a low GI of 49, and moderate cardiovascular concern from saturated fat in the meatballs. It is suitable for halal diets when beef or halal-certified pork alternatives are used. A standard serving is approximately 300 g, delivering around 327 kcal and 816 mg sodium. Substituting refined pasta with wholemeal or legume-based pasta and reducing sodium in the sauce are the primary dietary modifications recommended for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: How many calories in meatball pasta?
Is meatball pasta high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060078) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white pasta with tomato-based meat sauce, which reduces overall confidence for the GI component to medium; however, the nutrient profile confidence remains high. Overall record confidence is set to high based on the primary HPB source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 1.5 g per 100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable beef/pork meatball pasta dishes (USDA SR Legacy item 'Spaghetti with meat sauce'), as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately. This is an adapted estimate. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source or USDA equivalent for this composite dish): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite meatball pasta preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or confirmed in USDA SR Legacy for an exact match as of 2026. 3. GI value of 49 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white pasta with tomato-based meat sauce; the input record noted a confidence of 0.536 for this GI value, reflecting the analogous nature of the estimate. 4. Halal dietary flag set to true based on input cultural tags; this assumes halal-certified beef or pork alternatives are used — consumers should verify with the specific food outlet. 5. This is a Western dish; multilingual naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) apply only to Singaporean dishes per Project Veritas v2 rules. Meatball Pasta is a Western dish and does not require mandatory local-language names beyond English.
